system_description.overview: 11x AI Inc. is an AI-powered automation company specializing in autonomous 'digital workers' for go-to-market (GTM) teams. Its platform delivers enterprise-ready AI agents that operate 24/7 to research prospects, conduct outreach, qualify leads, and book meetings with minimal human oversight. The platform integrates with major CRMs and communication tools with end-to-end encryption.

products.Alice (AI Sales Development): Autonomous SDR that identifies prospects, researches accounts, and conducts personalized multichannel outreach (email, LinkedIn) to book qualified meetings.
products.Julian (AI Phone Agent): Manages inbound and outbound calls in natural, multilingual conversations, qualifies leads in real time, and schedules meetings. Newly added to SOC 2 scope during this review period.
products.Pipeline Automation: Automates prospect engagement and meeting scheduling with seamless integration into CRM systems such as Salesforce and HubSpot.

infrastructure.cloud_provider: AWS (carve-out method - AWS controls excluded from scope, AWS SOC 2 reviewed separately)
infrastructure.physical_presence: None - fully cloud-based, no physical offices or servers in scope. HQ at 677 Harrison Street, San Francisco, CA (co-working space)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Segmented VPCs with access control lists (ACLs). Network segmentation implemented to separate systems and data based on sensitivity and criticality (CC6.1.7).
network_architecture.api_gateway: Application gateway with firewalls configured on it (CC6.6.1)
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on the application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management. Firewall access restricted to authorized personnel only (CC6.6.1, CC6.6.2, CC6.6.3).
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS employed via AWS to identify and protect against threats in conjunction with security policy network settings. Intrusion detection system utilized to monitor and detect unauthorized access or anomalies (CC2.1.1).
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access is done through a bastion host with a virtual private network (VPN).
network_architecture.tls: TLS encryption using certificates from an established third-party certificate authority for web-based systems.
network_architecture.content_filtering: All systems are restricted to the internet with the content filtering system routed through the proxy server.
network_architecture.topology: Users connect via TLS to web-based systems. Remote admin access through bastion host + VPN. Segmented VPCs with ACLs protect internal resources. AWS IDS/IPS for threat detection. Application gateway with firewalls. Network diagram shows Alice and Julian architectures with STYTCH for auth, AWS S3/RDS for storage, CRM integrations (Salesforce, HubSpot, Zoho), and telephony (Twilio, WhatsApp) for Julian.

application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test and production environments. Changes developed and tested in separate environment before implementation (CC8.1.4).

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: STYTCH (for application auth/authorization, visible in Julian architecture diagram). AWS Password Policy for internal systems.
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1). MFA enforced for all remote access (CC6.1.1 test). Source code tool access requires MFA (CC8.1.3). Two-factor authentication required for employee email access.
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination of employment (CC6.1.4, CC6.1.8)
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Controlled through AWS Password policy with periodic forced changes, password expiry, and complexity requirements. Accounts disabled after limited number of unsuccessful logon attempts. Guest and anonymous logins not allowed. Unattended desktops locked within a time of inactivity.

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used to encrypt data at rest (CC6.1.5)
encryption.key_management: AWS KMS - cloud provider key management service to store and manage encryption keys. Access to production environment access keys restricted to authorized individuals (CC6.1.5, CC6.1.9).

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Source code management tool (specific vendor not named). Source code changes logged, time-stamped, and attributed to author. Access restricted to authorized users with MFA (CC8.1.3).
ci_cd_devops.ci_cd_tool: Not explicitly named. Internal tool used for tracking changes from initiation through deployment and validation (CC8.1.1).
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Repository branch rules configured to ensure every merge request to move change to production environment requires additional approval (CC8.1.2). Approval process followed prior to merging to master branch.
ci_cd_devops.iac: Baseline configurations retained within infrastructure as code. Configuration management tool used for implementation and rollback capability (CC7.1.1).
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management with monthly patch management reviews by CTO/Management. Workstations configured for automatic updates and scanned daily for patch compliance (CC7.5.1).

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named - monitoring tools used for continuous infrastructure performance monitoring (CC5.2.1)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Not named - logging enabled to monitor administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions at application and infrastructure level, changes to functions, security configurations, permissions, and roles (CC7.2.1).
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events. Monitoring tool generates alerts when specific predefined thresholds are met. Incident management process invoked for confirmed events (CC5.2.1, CC7.2.1).

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud infrastructure and data center services (subservice organization)
Cloud compute provider (visible in Julian architecture diagram)
Durable function queues for async processing
Authentication and authorization for application users
Telephony and voice for Julian AI Phone Agent
Voice synthesis for Julian real-time voice AI stack
AI/LLM responses for Julian voice AI stack

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and malware protection installed on all production servers, configured for continuous automatic scanning. Virus definitions updated weekly for production servers. Anti-virus and malware protection also installed on Mac workstations and laptops (CC6.8.1).
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications, with scan frequency adjusted as required. Remediation plan developed for critical and high vulnerabilities (CC7.1.2).
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party. Results reviewed by IT management. High/critical findings remediated and added to risk register (CC4.1.1).

untestable: CC2.2.3 - No significant changes to people, roles and responsibilities during engagement period
CC3.2.1 - Cybersecurity insurance could not be tested because there were no cyber incidents reported during the engagement period
CC6.5.2 - Customer data deletion upon termination could not be tested because there were no customer terminations during the engagement period
CC7.3.1 - Incident response could not be tested because there were no security incidents reported during the engagement period
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 - Physical access to facilities: AWS responsible for physical security of data centers, not in scope
A1.2 - Environmental protections: 11x operates from co-working space, environmental controls managed by building management/AWS
Processing Integrity - Determined not relevant to the system
Privacy - Determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: RGEC (Risk and Governance Executive Committee) meets semiannually to review internal controls, risk assessments, and compliance frameworks (CC2.1.2)
IT Leadership Committee meets weekly to review security activities and key performance metrics (CC3.1.1)
Formal vendor management with quarterly risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports (CC9.2.1)
Data classification policy established to categorize data by sensitivity and criticality (CC2.1.3)
Configuration management tool with infrastructure-as-code and rollback capability (CC7.1.1)
Branch protection rules requiring additional approval for production merges (CC8.1.2)

infrastructure.rationale: AWS with multi-AZ, VPC segmentation, and IaC for baseline configurations. However, single cloud provider, no CDN mentioned, no multi-region capability disclosed, and specific AWS regions not named. Additional cloud usage (Vercel, Inngest) adds some diversity but also complexity.

application_architecture.rationale: Hybrid architecture with separate products (Alice, Julian) using different tech stacks visible in diagrams. Branch protection and code review enforced. However, no programming languages, frameworks, or specific compute services named in report text. API style not disclosed. Architecture diagrams show reasonable separation of concerns but report text specifics are thin.

data_layer.rationale: AWS RDS and S3 explicitly shown in architecture diagrams. Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration. Data classification and retention policies in place. Per-tenant segregation confirmed (CC6.1.3). However, no caching layer, data warehouse, or additional database types mentioned.

security_posture.rationale: Strong security posture with IDS/IPS, firewalls, endpoint protection, anti-malware, MFA enforcement, annual pen testing, monthly vulnerability scanning, server hardening, cybersecurity insurance, and content filtering. Encryption at rest and in transit. Incident response plan with root cause analysis. No WAF explicitly mentioned. Specific security tool vendors not named. No SIEM named.

devops_maturity.rationale: IaC with configuration management and rollback capability. Branch protection requiring approval for production merges. Source code logging with timestamps and attribution. Separate dev/test and production environments. Emergency change process documented. However, specific CI/CD tool, source control platform, and container orchestration not named.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually. Annual testing of DR plans with different loss scenarios. Multi-AZ failover with daily backups and backup failure alerting. However, no RTO/RPO disclosed. No multi-region failover.

vendor_diversity.rationale: 11+ distinct third-party vendors identified across infrastructure (AWS, Vercel, Inngest), authentication (STYTCH), CRM (Salesforce, HubSpot, Zoho), telephony (Twilio, WhatsApp), AI/voice (ElevenLabs, FIXIE/Llama). Good diversity with no extreme single-vendor lock-in, though AWS is the primary infrastructure dependency.

overall.rationale: 11x demonstrates solid security and compliance for a growth-stage AI company with clean SOC 2 Type 2 opinion and zero exceptions. Good foundational controls including MFA, IDS/IPS, encryption, branch protection, BCDR testing, and vendor management. Key gaps: lack of specificity around tooling (no named CI/CD, monitoring, SIEM), no multi-region, undisclosed RTO/RPO, short 3-month audit period, and excluded Processing Integrity/Privacy criteria.

red_flags: Very short audit period (3 months: May-August 2025) - minimum viable period for Type 2, likely first SOC 2
No RTO/RPO targets disclosed despite having BCDR policy and testing
Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria excluded - notable for an AI company handling customer prospect data and making autonomous outreach
Cybersecurity insurance control (CC3.2.1) could not be operationally tested due to no incidents - insurance coverage itself should be independently verifiable
Julian product newly added to SOC 2 scope during this period - indicates rapidly expanding system boundary that may outpace security controls maturation
yellow_flags: Specific security and DevOps tooling not named throughout report (no named CI/CD, SIEM, monitoring, or APM tools) - makes independent verification difficult
No WAF explicitly mentioned despite having internet-facing AI services
No multi-region failover capability disclosed - single region dependency for a 24/7 AI service
No specific TLS version mentioned (should be TLS 1.2+ minimum)
Content filtering 'routed through proxy server' is vague and does not name the technology
4 controls untestable due to no qualifying events during the short audit period
CC6.4 (physical access) excluded - reasonable for cloud-only but HQ address is listed suggesting some physical presence
green_flags: Clean unqualified opinion with zero exceptions across all tested controls
MFA enforced comprehensively: production access, remote access, source code, and email
Infrastructure-as-code with configuration management and rollback capability
Branch protection requiring approval before production merges - developers cannot push directly to production
Annual external penetration testing with remediation tracking
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning with remediation plans for critical/high findings
BCDR plans tested annually with multiple loss scenarios and plan updates based on results
Daily backups with multi-AZ and backup failure alerting
Formal vendor management program with SOC report reviews and quarterly assessments for non-SOC vendors
Data classification policy implemented to categorize data by sensitivity
RGEC and IT Leadership Committee provide structured security governance
Access revocation within one business day of termination
Quarterly access reviews for all user accounts including administrative privileges
key_observations: Architecture diagrams (page 19) reveal significantly more technical detail than report text - Julian uses STYTCH for auth, AWS S3/RDS for storage, Vercel for compute, Inngest for durable function queues, Salesforce/HubSpot/Zoho for CRM, Twilio/WhatsApp for telephony, ElevenLabs for voice synthesis, and FIXIE proprietary Llama models for AI responses
11x runs proprietary LLM models (FIXIE/Llama) rather than relying solely on third-party LLM APIs - unusual for a company at this stage and carries both IP value and operational complexity
Julian product was added to SOC 2 scope during this audit period, suggesting rapid product expansion

Despite claiming 'end-to-end encryption' in system description overview, technical details only confirm TLS in transit and KMS at rest - true E2E encryption for an outbound calling/email platform would be architecturally unusual
Company operates with no physical offices in scope - simplifies security perimeter but increases dependency on cloud provider controls

Lean leadership: CEO Prabhav Jain with 4 direct reports (CTO Jeson Patel, Head of Customer Success Lauren Kennedy, VP Finance Connor Nowinski, Head of Talent Justin Swisher) - CTO as signing authority indicates technical leadership prominence

Report heavily reuses control descriptions across multiple trust criteria (e.g., CC1.4.1 appears under CC1.2, CC2.2, CC2.3, CC3.1, CC3.4) - common but makes it harder to assess true breadth of unique controls
Approximately 55 unique controls tested across CC1-CC9 sections with all passing - no exceptions is a strong result but short audit window limits confidence
